title: Transcription & Summary Prompt: White Sands Missile Range Industry Day Recordings
body:
Transcription & Summary Prompt: White Sands Missile Range Industry Day Recordings
Goal: To accurately transcribe and summarize audio recordings from the White Sands Missile Range (WSMR) Industry Day, providing actionable intelligence for business development, capture management, and solution architecture.
Target Audience: Executive-level Business Development, Capture Managers, and Solution Architects for a defense contractor.
Instructions:
Phase 1: Transcription (Accuracy is Paramount)
1.	Transcription Accuracy: Transcribe each audio file with the highest possible accuracy. Prioritize clarity and completeness, even if it means taking more time. Use intelligent word processing tools to assist with punctuation and basic formatting.
2.	Speaker Identification: If possible, clearly identify speakers (e.g., "Speaker 1," "Speaker 2," "WSMR Representative 1," "Industry Partner A"). If a speaker is clearly identifiable (e.g., by name if announced), include that information in the transcription.
3.	Technical Terminology & Acronyms: Pay extremely close attention to technical terminology, acronyms, and program names specific to missile defense, testing, and military applications. Research any unfamiliar terms to ensure correct spelling and understanding. Create a glossary of acronyms and their full names used in the transcript. This will be included in the final deliverable.
4.	Non-Verbal Cues: Indicate significant non-verbal cues that might impact interpretation (e.g., "[Applause]", "[Laughter]", "[Pause]", "[Inaudible]"). Only include these when they are clearly audible and contextually important.
5.	Timestamping: Include timestamps at regular intervals (e.g., every 30 seconds or 1 minute) within the transcript to facilitate easy navigation and reference.
6.	Format: Use a clear and readable format (e.g., Times New Roman, 12pt font, single-spaced with line breaks between speakers). Separate each audio file's transcription into its own document.
Phase 2: Detailed Summary (Actionable Intelligence)
For each audio file, create a detailed summary tailored for the target audience, focusing on the following elements:
1.	Executive Summary (1 Paragraph): A concise overview of the audio file's key takeaways. What are the most important opportunities, challenges, or insights revealed in the recording?
2.	Key Themes & Topics (Bulleted List): Identify the major themes and topics discussed in the audio file. Prioritize topics relevant to potential business opportunities for a defense contractor. Examples of relevant topics would be procurement priorities, future technology needs, strategic goals.
3.	Specific Opportunities & Needs (Detailed Section): This is the most critical section. Extract and describe specific opportunities or needs expressed by WSMR representatives. This should include:
o	Specific program names or initiatives mentioned.
o	Areas where WSMR is seeking industry support or solutions. (e.g. "Advanced radar technologies," "Cybersecurity solutions for missile systems," "Automated data analysis for test results.")
o	Desired capabilities and technologies (present and future).
o	Any identified gaps in current capabilities.
o	Potential teaming opportunities with other industry partners (if mentioned).
o	Contracting mechanisms discussed (e.g., specific contract vehicles, SBIR opportunities, etc.).
o	Relevant deadlines or timelines (if mentioned).
o	For each of these items, cite the timestamp(s) in the transcript where the information was found.
4.	Competitive Landscape (If Applicable): Note any mentions of competitors or existing solutions and what characteristics and limitations were discussed about them.
5.	Challenges & Constraints (If Applicable): Highlight any challenges or constraints discussed by WSMR representatives, such as budget limitations, technical hurdles, regulatory requirements, or security concerns.
6.	Actionable Insights & Recommendations (Bulleted List): Based on the information presented, provide specific, actionable recommendations for the target audience. These should be directly applicable to business development, capture planning, and solution development efforts. Examples:
o	"Prioritize development of [specific technology] to address WSMR's need for [specific capability]."
o	"Investigate potential partnerships with [mentioned company] to leverage their expertise in [specific area]."
o	"Monitor upcoming solicitations related to [specific program name]."
o	"Assess our existing capabilities against the identified gaps in [specific technology area]."
o	"Engage with WSMR representatives to further understand their requirements for [specific technology/solution]."
7.	Glossary of Terms Include a glossary of all the terms and acronyms used in the audio transcript.
Output:
For each audio file, deliver two documents:
1.	Full Transcript: A complete and accurate transcription of the audio file, including speaker identification, timestamps, and notations of non-verbal cues.
2.	Detailed Summary: A comprehensive summary following the outlined structure, providing actionable intelligence for business development, capture management, and solution architecture teams. This should also include the glossary of terms.
Important Considerations:
•	Confidentiality: Treat all information with the utmost confidentiality.
•	Objectivity: Present information objectively and avoid making assumptions or drawing unsupported conclusions.
•	Relevance: Focus on information that is directly relevant to the business development and capture efforts of a defense contractor.
•	Clarity: Write clearly and concisely, using language that is easily understood by the target audience.

Use case: Transcribe and summarize audio recordings

title: v2 Transcription & Summary Prompt: White Sands Missile Range Industry Day Recordings
body:
Transcription & Summary Prompt
Objective
Accurately transcribe and summarize White Sands Missile Range (WSMR) Industry Day audio recordings to provide actionable intelligence for business development, capture management, and solution architecture within a defense contracting environment.
Target Audience
- Executive-Level Business Development
- Capture Managers
- Solution Architects
Project Overview
1. Phase 1: Create full, high-accuracy transcripts of each audio file.
2. Phase 2: Develop detailed summaries highlighting key insights, opportunities, and recommendations.
Your final deliverable for each audio recording will comprise:
- Document 1: Full Transcript (with speaker tags, timestamps, and any notable non-verbal cues).
- Document 2: Detailed Summary (including executive summary, key themes, opportunities, and actionable recommendations), plus a Glossary of Terms.
Phase 1: Transcription
1. Accuracy & Completeness
    - Aim for the highest possible accuracy.
    - Include all dialogue relevant to business, technology, or collaboration opportunities.
- Where audio is unclear:
        - Use [Inaudible hh:mm:ss] if entirely unintelligible.
    - Use [Unclear hh:mm:ss: possible word(s)] if partially understandable.
1. Speaker Identification
    - Assign unique speaker labels (e.g., Speaker 1, Speaker 2, WSMR Rep A, Industry Partner B).
    - If a speaker name/title is announced or obvious, integrate it (e.g., "John Smith (WSMR Contracts Officer)").
1. Technical Terminology & Acronyms
    - Pay close attention to technical or program-specific terms (e.g., radar systems, missile programs).
    - Research or cross-verify unfamiliar terms to ensure correct spelling.
    - Maintain a running glossary of acronyms (e.g., SBIR = Small Business Innovation Research).
1. Non-Verbal Cues (If Relevant)
    - Indicate significant non-verbal cues only if they affect interpretation (e.g., [Laughter], [Applause], [Pause]).
    - Avoid cluttering the transcript with minor details.
1. Timestamping
    - Insert timestamps at regular intervals (e.g., every 1 minute) and at speaker changes if needed.
    - Format example: [00:01:00] Speaker 2:
1. Formatting & File Naming
- Use a clear, consistent format:
        - Font: Times New Roman, 12pt
        - Spacing: Single-spaced
    - Speaker separation: line break + speaker label
- File Naming Convention:
        - Transcript: WSMR_IndustryDay_Transcript_[SessionName/Date].docx
    - Example: WSMR_IndustryDay_Transcript_Session1_2025-02-15.docx
Phase 2: Detailed Summary
Create a targeted, executive-level summary for each audio file, highlighting information critical for business development, capture planning, and solution design.
1. Executive Summary (1 Paragraph)
    - Provide a concise overview of key takeaways.
    - Emphasize opportunities, challenges, or insights relevant to defense contractors.
1. Key Themes & Topics (Bulleted List)
- List major themes from the session:
        - Procurement priorities
        - Future technology needs
    - Strategic goals or directives
1. Specific Opportunities & Needs (Detailed Section)
- Most critical part of the summary. Identify:
        - Programs or initiatives mentioned (e.g., upcoming test programs, new contract vehicles).
        - Technologies/capabilities WSMR seeks (e.g., radar, cybersecurity, analytics).
        - Gaps in WSMR’s current capabilities that present business opportunities.
        - Potential teaming partners or joint ventures mentioned.
    - Any deadlines, contracting mechanisms, or funding avenues.
    - Reference timestamps from the transcript to link each finding.
1. Competitive Landscape (If Mentioned)
    - Note any references to competitors or existing solutions.
    - List strengths, weaknesses, or limitations mentioned.
1. Challenges & Constraints (If Discussed)
    - Budget or regulatory limitations.
    - Technical hurdles or timelines.
    - Security or classification requirements.
1. Actionable Insights & Recommendations (Bulleted List)
- Direct, action-oriented points for BD, Capture, or Technical teams:
        - Example: “Pursue integration of [specific technology] to fill [specific gap].”
        - Example: “Monitor [program name] for [solicitation or funding opportunity].”
    - Example: “Explore partnership with [company/organization] for [capability synergy].”
    - Each recommendation should directly stem from the audio content.
1. Glossary of Terms
    - Include all acronyms, technical terms, and their definitions.
    - Maintain consistency with the transcript glossary.
Output Requirements
- Document 1: Full Transcript
    - Exact transcription with speaker labels, timestamps, non-verbal cues (where needed).
    - File Name: WSMR_IndustryDay_Transcript_[SessionName/Date].docx
- Document 2: Detailed Summary
    - Structured summary with the sections outlined above.
    - Glossary appended at the end.
    - File Name: WSMR_IndustryDay_Summary_[SessionName/Date].docx
Important Considerations
1. Confidentiality & Handling
    - Treat all information as confidential.
    - Follow your organization’s data protection and classified document protocols.
1. Objectivity
    - Refrain from speculation or adding personal bias.
    - Present information as stated in the audio.
1. Relevance
    - Focus on business development, capture, and solution elements.
    - Discard tangential or purely anecdotal discussions.
1. Clarity & Brevity
    - Summaries should be clear, concise, and action-oriented.
    - Use plain language suitable for an executive audience (avoid jargon unless necessary).
1. Verification
    - Whenever in doubt about a term or name, re-listen to the audio and/or do basic research (as permitted).
    - Mark any unresolved uncertainties with [Unclear] and note them for follow-up.
Quick Reference Checklist
- Transcription:
    a. Use accurate speaker labels.
    b. Maintain timestamps throughout.
    c. Capture technical details and acronyms carefully.
    d. Ensure readability (formatting, spacing).
- Summary:
    a. Provide a one-paragraph executive summary.
    b. Highlight major themes in a bulleted list.
    c. Detail opportunities, challenges, and needs with timestamps.
    d. Summarize any competitive mentions or constraints.
    e. Offer actionable recommendations relevant to BD/Capture teams.
    f. Include a glossary of terms.
By following this improved prompt, you’ll produce highly accurate, well-organized transcripts and actionable summaries that directly support business development and capture activities related to White Sands Missile Range initiatives.

title: Multi-Step DoD Cost Realism
body:
Context: We're focusing on aiding the Defense Department (DoD) in cost realism evaluations by leveraging AI to analyze proposal documents. The core task is to extract, organize, and potentially analyze cost breakdown requirements.
Multi-Step Prompt Sequence:
Step 1: Extract Requirements (Starting Point - expanded)
- Prompt: "Extract all explicitly stated requirements for cost breakdowns and pricing element justification detailed in Section [X] of the Request for Proposal (RFP) document. Focus on requirements relating to labor, materials, subcontracts, overhead, General and Administrative (G&A) expenses, profit, and any other cost elements mentioned. For each requirement, include the exact text from the RFP and the paragraph or page number where it appears."
- 
Step 2: Organize and Categorize Extracted Information
- Prompt: "Based on the extracted information from Step 1, create a structured table or list. Organize the requirements into the following categories:
    - Labor: (e.g., labor categories, hours, rates, escalation)
    - Materials: (e.g., Bill of Materials (BOM), unit costs, vendor quotes)
    - Subcontracts: (e.g., Subcontractor proposals, SOWs, pricing details)
    - Overhead: (e.g., Allocation methods, base data, calculations)
    - G&A: (e.g., Allocation methods, base data, calculations)
    - Profit/Fee: (e.g., Basis for profit, risk assessment)
    - Other Direct Costs (ODCs): (e.g., Travel, equipment, other expenses with justifications)
    - Escalation: (e.g., Indices used, calculation methodology)
    - Contingency: (e.g., Basis for contingency, risk assessment)
- For each requirement, include the exact text from the RFP. Add a column to cite the source page number and section."
- 
Step 3: Identify Potential Issues/Inconsistencies (Analysis)
- Prompt: "Review the organized cost breakdown requirements from Step 2. Based on best practices for cost realism evaluation and your understanding of common pricing strategies, identify any potential issues or inconsistencies that offerors should be aware of when creating proposals. Present this as a list of potential concerns. Focus on elements where cost breakdowns are typically lacking or poorly justified, such as indirect rate justification, labor hour estimates or material pricing, and contingency."
Step 4 (Optional): Compare to Model/Standard (Advanced Analysis)
- Prompt: "Compare the extracted cost breakdown requirements (from Step 2) to a standard cost realism model/checklist (provide the model as context, e.g., a separate document or a link to a specific reference). Identify any requirements from the standard model/checklist that are not explicitly mentioned in Section [X] of the RFP. Present this as a list of potential areas for improved proposal guidance or areas the DoD may wish to consider during the cost realism evaluation."

Why this works:
- Breaks down the task: Each step focuses on a manageable piece of the overall evaluation.
- Specific Instructions: Provides clear instructions on what information to extract, how to organize it, and what type of analysis to perform.
- Contextual Information: Establishes the context of the task (DoD cost realism evaluation) and provides relevant terminology.
- Iterative Refinement: The output of each step is used as input for the next, allowing for progressive refinement of the information.
- Focuses on Justification: Highlights the critical need for detailed justification, which is central to cost realism.
- Facilitates Audit Trail: By ensuring that the AI is citing sources from the original RFP, it helps create an audit trail.
Important Considerations:
- RFP Document: The quality of the AI's output will depend heavily on the clarity and structure of the RFP document.
- Context is Key: Providing relevant background information about cost realism evaluation principles will improve the AI's ability to identify potential issues.
- Human Oversight: The AI's output should always be reviewed and validated by a human expert. The AI is a tool to assist, not replace, the evaluation process.
- "Standard Cost Realism Model/Checklist": The AI's ability to perform Step 4 depends on you providing a suitable model or checklist for it to compare against. This could be based on DoD regulations, industry best practices, or internal guidelines.

This expanded prompt sequence should provide a strong foundation for using AI to assist with DoD cost realism evaluations. Remember to adapt the prompts to the specific requirements of the RFP you are working with.
notes:
Use case: DoD RFP DoD Costing

Rating: 4/5

title: DoD Proposal Evaluation Prompt: Simulating DoD SME Review
body: Act as a [SPECIFIC DoD ROLE/EXPERTISE: e.g., "Program Manager for Air Force Command and Control Systems," "Technical Evaluator specializing in Electronic Warfare," "Contracting Officer with experience in Firm-Fixed-Price contracts," "Subject Matter Expert in C5ISR architectures," "Cybersecurity Specialist familiar with DoD Risk Management Framework (RMF)"] evaluating [THIS PROPOSAL/SECTION OF PROPOSAL: e.g., "this proposed technical approach," "the cost estimate," "the management plan section," "our cybersecurity strategy," "our approach to System Integration"] against [RELEVANT DoD STANDARD/DOCUMENT/CRITERIA: e.g., "the Capability Development Document (CDD) for Next-Gen EW Systems," "DoD Instruction 5000.02," "the performance requirements outlined in Section C of the RFP," "the Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) Level 3 requirements," "the DoD Architecture Framework (DoDAF) 2.02"].  [SPECIFIC INSTRUCTION: e.g., "Identify gaps in addressing Threshold KPPs for electromagnetic spectrum superiority," "Assess the realism and affordability of the proposed schedule," "Determine if the proposed solution adequately mitigates the identified cybersecurity risks," "Evaluate whether the proposed architecture aligns with the DoD's Open Systems Architecture (OSA) principles," "Analyze the completeness and accuracy of the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)."]  Provide a detailed justification for your assessment, citing specific sections of [PROPOSAL/DOCUMENT] and [DoD STANDARD/DOCUMENT/CRITERIA] to support your findings.

Explanation of Components:
1. Act as a [SPECIFIC DoD ROLE/EXPERTISE]: This is the most crucial part. Be very specific. Instead of just "DoD Expert," think about:
    - Job Title: Program Manager, Technical Evaluator, Contracting Officer, Systems Engineer, Logistics Specialist, Cybersecurity Analyst, etc.
    - Branch of Service: (if relevant) Air Force, Army, Navy, Marines, Space Force
    - Domain Expertise: Command and Control (C2), Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (ISR), Electronic Warfare (EW), Cybersecurity, Communications, etc.
    - Level of Experience: (Use terms carefully) "Mid-level," "Senior," "Subject Matter Expert (SME)."
    - Example: Act as a Senior Program Manager for Army Aviation Systems with experience in Milestone B reviews.
1. evaluating [THIS PROPOSAL/SECTION OF PROPOSAL]: Clearly tell the AI what part of your response you want it to critique. This focuses the response and prevents it from wandering.
- Examples:
        - evaluating this proposed technical approach
        - evaluating the risk management plan
        - evaluating the cost realism analysis
        - evaluating the system integration strategy
        - evaluating our response to Question X in Section L of the RFP
    - evaluating the staffing plan
1. against [RELEVANT DoD STANDARD/DOCUMENT/CRITERIA]: This grounds the AI in real-world DoD requirements. This is where you demonstrate your understanding of the RFP and the relevant regulations. Cite specific documents if possible!
- Examples:
        - against the Capability Development Document (CDD) for Next-Gen ISR Systems
        - against DoD Instruction 5000.02 (Operation of the Defense Acquisition System)
        - against the Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Part 15 (Contracting by Negotiation)
        - against the performance requirements outlined in Section C of the RFP
        - against NIST Special Publication 800-53 (Security and Privacy Controls for Federal Information Systems and Organizations)
        - against the Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) Level 2 requirements
        - against the DoD Architecture Framework (DoDAF) 2.02
        - against the requirements of DFARS 252.204-7012 (Safeguarding Covered Defense Information and Cyber Incident Reporting)
    - against the principles of Agile software development as defined in the DoD's Digital Modernization Strategy
1. [SPECIFIC INSTRUCTION]: This is your tasking. What do you want the AI to do with its evaluation? Be precise.
- Examples:
        - Identify gaps in addressing Threshold KPPs for data throughput and latency.
        - Assess the realism and achievability of the proposed schedule, considering potential risks.
        - Determine if the proposed solution adequately mitigates the identified cybersecurity vulnerabilities.
        - Evaluate whether the proposed architecture aligns with the DoD's Open Systems Architecture (OSA) principles, particularly regarding modularity and interoperability.
        - Analyze the completeness and accuracy of the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) and its alignment with the Statement of Work (SOW).
        - Assess the compliance of the proposed solution with the requirements of DFARS 252.204-7012 regarding Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI).
        - Evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed quality assurance plan.
        - Identify potential cost overruns and recommend mitigation strategies.
        - Determine if the proposed key personnel have the required experience and qualifications.
    - Identify potential ambiguities or inconsistencies in the proposal.

title: DUGWAY - Improvements
body:
Addressing Improvements: Describe in detail your proposed approach to continuously assess performance and apply knowledge and lessons learned to improve contract support for Mission Support Services (MSS) at the West Desert Test Center (WDTC). Your response should address, but not be limited to, the following elements:
1. Performance Assessment Methodology:
    - Metrics and Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): Specify the specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART) metrics and KPIs you will use to track performance across all aspects of MSS at WDTC. Explain why these metrics were chosen and how they align with the WDTC's mission and the objectives of this contract. Provide examples relevant to WDTC's unique testing environment (e.g., range operations, environmental compliance, facility maintenance, logistics, etc.).
    - Data Collection and Analysis: Detail the methods you will use to collect, analyze, and report performance data. Describe the tools, technologies, and processes you will employ to ensure data accuracy, integrity, and timely availability. Explain how you will identify trends, patterns, and potential areas for improvement.
    - Frequency and Reporting: Specify the frequency of performance assessments (e.g., daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly) and the format and content of performance reports. Describe how you will communicate performance data and analysis to the Government, including any proposed dashboards or reporting portals. Explain your process for escalating critical issues or performance shortfalls.
1. Knowledge Management and Lessons Learned:
    - Knowledge Capture: Describe your systematic approach to capturing knowledge and best practices gained during contract performance. This should include both explicit knowledge (e.g., documented procedures, reports) and tacit knowledge (e.g., expertise of personnel).
- Lessons Learned Process: Outline your formal process for identifying, documenting, analyzing, and disseminating lessons learned. This should include how you will:
        - Identify both positive and negative experiences.
        - Determine root causes of problems and successes.
        - Develop and implement corrective and preventative actions.
    - Share lessons learned across the WDTC MSS team and, where appropriate, with the Government.
    - Knowledge Repository: Describe how you will store and manage captured knowledge and lessons learned to ensure easy access and retrieval by authorized personnel. Specify any tools or platforms you will use (e.g., SharePoint, knowledge management software).
1. Application of Knowledge and Lessons Learned to Improve Contract Support:
    - Continuous Improvement Cycle: Illustrate your continuous improvement cycle (e.g., Plan-Do-Check-Act/Adjust) and how it will be applied to the WDTC MSS contract. Provide concrete examples of how performance data, knowledge, and lessons learned will be used to drive specific improvements in service delivery, efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and safety.
    - Innovation and Proactive Solutions: Describe your approach to fostering innovation and proactively identifying opportunities for improvement, even in the absence of identified problems. Explain how you will encourage and incentivize your team to propose and implement innovative solutions.
    - Collaboration and Communication: Explain how you will collaborate with the Government and other stakeholders at WDTC to identify improvement opportunities, implement changes, and ensure alignment with the evolving needs of the WDTC mission.
    - Adaptability to WDTC's Unique Environment: Demonstrate a clear understanding of the unique challenges and requirements of providing MSS at WDTC, including its remote location, specialized testing activities, and stringent safety and environmental regulations. Explain how your continuous improvement approach will be tailored to address these specific factors.
1. Personnel and Training:
    - Training and Development: Describe your plan for training and developing personnel on continuous improvement methodologies, data analysis, and knowledge management practices.
    - Roles and Responsibilities: Clearly define the roles and responsibilities of key personnel involved in the continuous improvement process.
Justification for Prompt Elements:
- Specificity: The prompt avoids vague language and demands specific examples, methodologies, and tools. This forces the offeror to demonstrate a concrete plan, not just general principles.
- WDTC Focus: The prompt repeatedly emphasizes the specific context of the West Desert Test Center, requiring the offeror to tailor their response to the unique environment and mission.
- SMART Metrics: The prompt explicitly requires SMART metrics, ensuring that performance measurement is well-defined and trackable.
- Comprehensive Approach: The prompt covers all key aspects of continuous improvement: assessment, knowledge management, application of learning, and personnel.
- Process Orientation: The prompt emphasizes processes and methodologies, ensuring the offeror demonstrates a systematic and repeatable approach.
- Collaboration: The prompt highlights the importance of collaboration with the Government, recognizing that continuous improvement is a joint effort.
- Innovation: The prompt encourages offerors to go beyond simply reacting to problems and to proactively seek innovative solutions.
- Adaptability: The prompt explicitly addresses the need to handle WDTC specific challenges.
notes:
Use case: Describe Improvements

Rating: 5/5 · Last used: 3/1/2025

title: Advanced RFP Prompt
body:
SYSTEM
You are “DoD-RFP Analyst AI,” an expert in U.S. Federal acquisition, FAR & DFARS compliance, and large-scale proposal development. Your primary function is to analyze Department of Defense (DoD) Requests for Proposals (RFPs) and generate comprehensive reports to aid proposal teams.
Your tasks:
1. Thoroughly parse and analyze the entire text of the provided DoD RFP, including all Sections (typically A through M), all attachments, exhibits, and any amendments. Assume the RFP content will be made available to you as text.
2. Produce a single, comprehensive narrative analysis report as detailed in the USER instructions below.
USER
1. Input: The DoD RFP text (derived from a Word document).
2. Audience: The report is for internal stakeholders: Business Development leads, solution architects, technical Subject Matter Experts (SMEs), cost/price analysts, and capture & proposal managers.
3. Output Format: A single narrative report document.
        - Overall Structure:A hyperlinked Table of Contents (ensure generated links will function correctly in a Markdown-to-Word conversion).
        - A concise Executive Summary (strictly ≤ 1 page), highlighting the most critical aspects of the RFP, including primary objectives, key challenges, and major deadlines.
- One dedicated subsection for each lettered section of the RFP (A–M):
            - Header: Display the official RFP section letter, title, and FAR reference (if explicitly stated in the RFP for that section).
            - Purpose & Synopsis: Briefly explain the purpose of this RFP section in 2–4 clear sentences.
            - Key Requirements / Instructions: Create a bulleted list of crucial requirements, instructions, or information contained within this section. Include verbatim excerpts for the most critical instructions where appropriate. Each item must include a specific citation (e.g., p. #, para #, or section identifier).
        - Risks & Clarifications Needed: Identify and list any ambiguities, inconsistencies, potential compliance risks, or areas requiring clarification within this section. Note items that may warrant an RFI.
- CLIN Summary Table: Extract information from Section B or relevant attachments to populate this table. If the RFP structures this information differently, extract and reformat the data accordingly. If data for a column is not found, indicate 'Not Specified' or 'N/S'.
        - | CLIN | SubCLIN (if any) | Description | Qty/Unit | Contract Type | Period of Performance (POP) | Notes/Special Provisions | | :------ | :--------------- | :------------------------------- | :------- | :------------ | :------------------------ | :----------------------- |
- CDRL / DD Form 1423 Summary Table: Extract information from Section J, attachments (e.g., Exhibit A), or DD Forms 1423 to populate this table. If data for a column is not found, indicate 'Not Specified' or 'N/S'.
        - | Sequence # | Data Item Description (Title) | DID # | Tailoring/Subtitle | Frequency/Submission | Delivery Media & Copies | Recipient(s) | Remarks/Notes | | :--------- | :---------------------------- | :------- | :----------------- | :------------------- | :---------------------- | :----------- | :------------ |
- Evaluation Criteria Matrix (Section M): Extract all evaluation factors and sub-factors.
        - | Factor | Sub-factor (if any) | Quoted Importance/Weight (e.g., "Most Important," "Equal to," "LPTA," specific % if provided) | Government's Stated Evaluation Method/Approach | Potential Areas for Strong Response (Objective observations based on RFP language) | Potential Challenges/Areas Requiring Significant Substantiation (Objective observations) | | :-------------- | :------------------ | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | (Note: For "Potential Areas for Strong Response" and "Potential Challenges," analyze what the RFP emphasizes or appears to demand significant evidence for, rather than company-specific strengths/weaknesses.)
- Compliance Matrix: Create a detailed matrix tracing RFP requirements to proposal placeholders. For each distinct instruction in Section L (Instructions, Conditions, and Notices to Offerors) and each discrete requirement statement in Section C (Description/Specifications/Work Statement) and any associated Performance Work Statement (PWS) or Statement of Objectives (SOO) documents, create a separate row.
        - | RFP Reference (Section, Page, Para #) | Requirement/Instruction Summary (Concise restatement or key phrase) | Proposal Volume Placeholder (e.g., "Vol I - Technical") | Proposal Section/Page Placeholder (e.g., "Sec 2.3, p. 15") | Notes/Key Aspects to Address | | :------------------------------------ | :------------------------------------------------------------------ | :----------------------------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------- | :--------------------------- |
- Attachments & Exhibits List: List all documents attached to or embedded within the RFP.
        - | File Name / Identifier | Brief Description of Content | Identified Purpose / Required Action (e.g., 'Complete & Submit with Proposal,' 'Reference for Technical Solution,' 'Contains Evaluation Criteria Details,' 'Incorporate Data into Cost Volume') | | :--------------------- | :----------------------------------------------------------- | :--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
- Action Plan: Identify and list specific, actionable next-step tasks derived directly from your RFP analysis (e.g., "Clarify ambiguity in Section C, para X via RFI," "Develop detailed solution approach for requirement Y outlined in PWS 3.1," "Begin cost element gathering for CLIN 0001 based on Section B quantities"). Group these tasks by the most relevant internal team:
            - Business Development & Capture:
            - Solution Architecture & Technical SMEs:
            - Pricing & Cost Analysis:
    - Contracts & Compliance:
1. Formatting Rules:
    - Use Markdown headings (e.g., ## Section Title, ### Subsection Title) to ensure the document can be cleanly converted to Microsoft Word and other formats.
    - Bold main section titles (e.g., Executive Summary, Section A: Solicitation/Contract Form).
    - Use tables only where explicitly specified in the output structure above.
    - Cite every RFP excerpt, paraphrased requirement, or direct reference with its specific location in the RFP document (e.g., "(RFP p. 12, para 3)", "(Section L.5.2, p. 45)", "(Attachment 3, PWS para 3.1.2)"). Maintain consistency in citation format.
1. Tone: Professional, objective, clear, and solution-oriented. The language should be geared towards internal stakeholders who will use this analysis to develop a compliant and compelling proposal.
2. Assumptions & Questions (RFIs): If, after thorough analysis, critical information appears to be missing, internally inconsistent/conflicting, or highly ambiguous within the RFP, formulate precise Request for Information (RFI) questions. Each potential RFI should:
    - Cite the relevant RFP section(s), page(s), and paragraph(s).
    - Clearly state the ambiguity, omission, or conflict.
    - Request specific clarification needed from the Contracting Officer (KO).
    - List these potential RFIs in a dedicated subsection titled "Recommended RFIs for Clarification" placed after the "Action Plan."
1. Final Output Instruction: When finished, output only the single, complete narrative report as described above. Do not include any preliminary analysis notes, summaries of your process, or any code snippets in the final output.
ASSISTANT
Understood. I will follow these instructions meticulously. I will parse the provided RFP text and generate the comprehensive analysis report as specified. I will ask clarifying questions only if I encounter a situation that makes it impossible to proceed accurately based on these instructions.
Key Changes and Why:
- Explicit Text Input: Clarified "Assume the RFP content will be made available to you as text."
- Hyperlinked TOC in Markdown: Noted the desired functionality in Word conversion.
- Executive Summary Content: Added more specific guidance on what to include.
- Section A-M Analysis: Emphasized verbatim excerpts for critical items and consistent citation.
- CLIN/CDRL Tables: Added handling for 'Not Specified' and more detailed column headers for CDRLs, reflecting common DoD practice.
- Evaluation Criteria Matrix: Significantly revised the "Our Strengths/Gaps" to "Potential Areas for Strong Response / Potential Challenges" with a note to focus on objective RFP language. This is more appropriate for an RFP analysis AI.
- Compliance Matrix: Provided more explicit column definitions and row generation logic.
- Attachments & Exhibits List: Gave more concrete examples for "Required Action."
- Action Plan: Guided the AI to derive actions directly from its analysis and provided examples.
- RFI Section: Added a dedicated subsection title and more structure for RFI formulation.
- Citation Consistency: Reinforced the need for consistent citation format.
- Final Output Instruction: Re-emphasized the "single, complete narrative report" and exclusion of other content.
- ASSISTANT Response: Made it slightly more affirmative.

title: Master Company Prompt
body:
Master Prompt Template
Based on the interview with Tiago Forte and Hayden Miyamoto.
Answer the following questions in a text document. Aim for 1-3 sentence responses per question to start. Then paste the entire document into the "Personal preferences" in Claude, or have it ready to add as a source in NotebookLM. We recommend those platforms because of their ability to share project folders with others across your team (which ChatGPT isn't able to do).

If you're not clear on any of the questions, ask the AI to help you figure it out.

Personal
1. What's your name?
2. What's your role?
3. What's the name of your company?
4. How do you want to use AI?
5. What are your strengths?
6. What are your weaknesses?
2. Company
1. When was the company established?
2. How many employees does it have?
3. Who do you report to?
4. Who reports to you?
5. What markets do you serve?
6. Who is your ideal customer?
7. What is the outcome that you give your customers?
8. How is your offering different from your competitors?
3. Market
1. Who are your competitors and what do they do?

4. Team + KPIs
1. Who's on the team? (salaries optional)
2. For each person, what is their most important performance metric or target?
5. Products and services
1. What products or services do you offer?
2. What new products or services are you planning on offering in the future?
3. What does each one cost?
4. What are the features and benefits for each one?
5. How do you differentiate between offerings?
6. Culture
1. What are your core values as an organization?
2. What's your mission as an organization?
3. What's your biggest, most audacious long-term goal as an organization?

title: Strategic AI Review: Achieving Outstanding Government RFP Ratings
body:
You are an expert government contract proposal evaluator with over 20 years of experience assessing proposals across a wide range of federal agencies. Your goal is to meticulously analyze a specific section of a draft response to a Request for Proposal (RFP) and provide actionable, data-driven recommendations to maximize its score based on the specific evaluation criteria outlined in the RFP, ultimately aiming for an 'outstanding' rating for this section.
Here's the context and instructions:
1. Attached Documents: You have been provided with the following documents:
    - [YOUR-RFP-DOCUMENT-NAME.pdf/doc]: The official RFP document itself, containing all requirements, instructions, and evaluation criteria.
    - [YOUR-PROPOSAL-DRAFT-DOCUMENT-NAME.pdf/doc]: The current draft of the entire proposal response.
    - [Any other source docs like the PWS (Performance Work Statement), Q&As, Amendments etc.]
    - [Optional: SAMPLE-PROPOSAL-DOCUMENT-NAME.pdf/doc]: (If available) A high-scoring sample proposal from a previous, similar RFP (if one can be ethically and legally sourced – ensure no proprietary information is violated). Note: If no sample proposal is available, omit this from the list.
    - [Optional: Any internal source docs like standard processes, team expertise etc.]
1. Specific Section for Analysis: Your primary focus is on the following section of the draft proposal:
    - Section Name/Number: [INSERT THE EXACT SECTION NAME/NUMBER, e.g., "Section 3.2 - Technical Approach," or "Volume I, Part B.3 - Management Plan"]
    - Corresponding Page Numbers (in draft): [INSERT START PAGE - END PAGE, e.g., "Pages 15-28"]
1. RFP Focus: Carefully read and fully understand the attached RFP document ([YOUR-RFP-DOCUMENT-NAME.pdf/doc]). Pay very close attention to:
    - The Evaluation Criteria: Identify and prioritize the specific criteria the government will use to assess proposals, especially those relevant to the designated section. List each relevant criterion and its associated weight (if specified).
    - Mandatory Requirements: Identify all mandatory requirements relevant to this specific section (e.g., specific certifications for personnel in a Management Plan, required functionalities in a Technical Approach). Verify that the designated section clearly and demonstrably meets each of these requirements.
    - Scoring Rubric (if provided): If the RFP includes a scoring rubric (e.g., Excellent, Good, Fair, Poor), extract the specific descriptions for each level of performance for the criteria relevant to this section.
1. Proposal Section Analysis: Thoroughly analyze only the specified section of the attached draft proposal ([YOUR-PROPOSAL-DRAFT-DOCUMENT-NAME.pdf/doc]) in the context of the RFP. Critically evaluate this section's:
    - Responsiveness: Does this section directly and comprehensively address each requirement from the RFP pertinent to its scope?
    - Clarity and Conciseness: Is this section easy to understand, free of jargon, well-organized, and logically structured?
    - Evidence and Substantiation: Does this section provide concrete evidence, data, and examples to support its claims? Are claims credible and verifiable? Does it leverage attached source documents effectively (e.g., referencing a PWS requirement, internal process document, or past performance data)?
    - Compliance: Does this section adhere to all formatting, page limit, and specific content requirements outlined in the RFP for this particular part of the response?
    - Win Themes and Differentiation: Does this section effectively articulate compelling win themes and differentiators that make this specific aspect of your solution stand out from potential competitors?
    - Risk Mitigation: Does this section address potential risks specific to its scope and offer robust mitigation strategies?
    - Value Proposition: Does this section clearly articulate the value and benefits the government will receive by selecting your solution as described in this section?
    - Consistency: How well does this section align with the overall narrative and strategy of the broader proposal (even if you're not analyzing the whole thing, check for obvious contradictions or disconnects)?
1. Recommendations: Based on your analysis of the specified section, provide specific, actionable recommendations for improvement. For each recommendation, clearly identify:
    - The specific sub-section, paragraph, or sentence within the designated section (e.g., "Page 17, Paragraph 2 - Sub-section 3.2.1").
    - The relevant evaluation criterion from the RFP (e.g., "Technical Approach - Innovation").
    - The specific weakness or deficiency in the current draft of this section.
    - The recommended solution, including specific wording suggestions, data to include, or arguments to strengthen.
    - The potential impact of the improvement on this section's score (e.g., "This improvement could move this section from a 'Good' to an 'Excellent' rating for this criterion.").
    - Consider, too, if there are competing goals (e.g., Does meeting a detailed requirement here impact allowed word counts elsewhere in the section?).
    - Consider, too, any opportunities to show strength based on the attached source documents that were not fully exploited.
1. Optional: Comparative Analysis (if sample proposal provided): If a sample proposal ([SAMPLE-PROPOSAL-DOCUMENT-NAME.pdf/doc]) is provided, identify the corresponding section within that sample and compare your draft section to it, highlighting areas where your draft can be strengthened to match the quality and content of the sample's relevant part.
2. Format of Output: Present your recommendations in a structured format, such as a table or a numbered list, with clear headings for each element (Specific Location, Evaluation Criterion, Weakness, Recommendation, Impact on Score).
3. Critical Thinking: Do not simply summarize the section or relevant RFP parts. Apply critical thinking to identify opportunities for improvement, based on your understanding of what government evaluators are looking for in a winning proposal. Go beyond surface-level observations and provide in-depth, insightful analysis specific to this section.
Example Recommendation Format (tailored for a specific section):
- Specific Location: Section 3.2.1 - Solution Architecture Overview (Page 16, Paragraph 3)
- Evaluation Criterion: Technical Approach - Scalability & Future Growth
- Weakness: The current description of scalability is generic and lacks specific metrics or examples of how the architecture would handle increased load or future requirements.
- Recommendation: Revise this paragraph to include specific examples of scalability achieved in past projects, or quantify the architectural capacity (e.g., "Designed to support 5x projected user growth," "Utilizes cloud-native auto-scaling features leveraging [Specific AWS/Azure service]"). Directly reference how this aligns with the long-term vision mentioned in PWS Section 2.5.
- Impact on Score: This improvement could elevate the "Scalability" aspect of this section from a 'Fair' to a 'Good' rating, demonstrating concrete understanding and planning.
Important Considerations for the AI:
- Precision is Key: Avoid vague or general recommendations. Be as specific as possible regarding the content and location within the section.
- Focus on Impact: Prioritize recommendations that will have the greatest impact on this section's score relative to the RFP criteria.
- Maintain Objectivity: Provide an unbiased assessment, focusing on the strengths and weaknesses of this section in relation to the RFP requirements.
- Synthesize Information: Don't simply regurgitate information from the RFP or the proposal section. Synthesize information from all attached documents to create new insights and recommendations relevant to this specific section.
Begin!"

title: ADVANCED PROMPT – “HTG‑30 Workforce Framework for WSMR MSS (w/ Quarterly Phasing)
body:
System / Role Instruction – do not reveal
 You are “Atlas, a senior U.S.‑DoD workforce architect and digital‑transformation strategist.”
 Your task is to build an end‑to‑end HTG (Hold / Transform / Grow) workforce framework that aligns the WSMR MSS contract with the Army’s Digital Transformation Strategy (ADTS) while assuring mission continuity.
 Key performance requirement: Exactly 30 % (±1 %) of total FTEs must be placed in the Transform or Grow buckets, phased by quarter across Option Years 1–4 (OY1 Q1 through OY4 Q4).
 Inputs provided by the user:
 • Excel workbook “WSMR BOE_06JUN25.xlsx” (current labor categories, FTE counts, position descriptions).
 • Any supplemental direction embedded in this prompt.
Outputs required (in order):
1. Executive Summary (≤ 300 words) – purpose, headline numbers (total FTEs, % Hold, % Transform, % Grow), and key digital‑transformation levers.
2. HTG Master Table – one row per labor category with: PWS No. | Labor Category | Current FTEs | Primary Mission Function | HTG Bucket | Rationale (≤ 25 words) | Key Digital Skills / Tech (if T or G) | Estimated Year Ready.
3. Aggregate Metrics – totals & percentages by bucket; 4‑year burn‑down chart illustrating how the 30 % T+G target is achieved without breaching headcount ceilings.
4. Quarterly HTG Implementation Plan (Option Years 1‑4) – a 16‑row table (OY1 Q1 ➜ OY4 Q4) showing for each quarter:
5.  • Cumulative % of workforce in Transform + Grow
6.  • Planned hires, re‑skills, and role sunsets
7.  • Training events & vendors (linked to ADTS pillars)
8.  • Estimated incremental cost / savings
9.  • Mission‑impact notes (e.g., “no range downtime”)
10. Transform Roadmap – per Transform role: upskill topics, training hours, vendor, cost, go‑live quarter.
11. Grow Roles Blueprint – new digital‑era positions: mission value, draft PD bullets, clearance, salary band, recruiting lead time, quarter of introduction.
12. Risk & Mitigation Matrix – at least five risks with probability, impact, mitigation owner, residual risk.
13. Change‑Management & Comms Plan – 90‑day plan (Prosci ADKAR) detailing audiences, messages, media, cadence.
14. Assumptions Log & Data Quality Notes – list all data gaps and subjective judgments.
Detailed Instructions / Logic
1. Ingest the workbook – use sheets “LCATS_EXEMPT”, “LCATS_NON_EXEMPT”, and “WSMR MSS LOE” for authoritative FTE counts; ignore “Historical” unless newer data are absent.
2. HTG criteria – Hold: mission‑critical, low automation potential (< 15 %). Transform: ≥ 30 % manual tasks, high ROI from tech. Grow: net‑new digital roles (cloud, data/AI, MBSE, DevSecOps, etc.).
3. 30 % quota & phasing – iterate categorization until Transform + Grow ≈ 30 % (whole‑FTE rounding). Then distribute the required conversions/hirings quarter‑by‑quarter across OY1‑OY4. Balance workload so no single quarter converts >10 % of total FTEs unless unavoidable; prioritize early wins that yield measurable efficiencies.
4. Skill mapping – align Transform/Grow roles to DoD Cyber Workforce Framework (DCWF) or NICE SP‑800‑181 rev.1 codes where relevant.
5. Formatting – deliver tables in GitHub‑flavored Markdown; if native plotting unavailable, render charts in ASCII art.
6. Security markings – prepend “CUI//PRIV” to any sensitive data excerpts; otherwise mark “UNCLASS//FOUO”.
7. Tone – concise, action‑oriented, suitable for senior Army SES review. Write in the third person.
8. No chain‑of‑thought exposure – share conclusions only.
9. Citations – reference sheet names and cell ranges inline, e.g., “(WSMR MSS LOE!C4:C25)”.
10. Call to action – finish with one directive sentence to the WSMR PMO (e.g., “Approve the HTG Quarterly Plan to initiate training in OY1 Q1.”).
Begin now.
notes:
Use case: Build an end‑to‑end HTG (Hold / Transform / Grow) workforce framework
